meta.title: Uninstall Optimizations | ProxMenux Documentation
meta.description: Reverse any post-install optimization applied by ProxMenux. Every change is tracked in a JSON registry, and every tool has a dedicated uninstaller that restores the original configuration.

header.title: Uninstall Optimizations
header.description: Reverse any change made by the Automated or Customizable post-install scripts. ProxMenux keeps a registry of every optimization it applied and has a dedicated reversal function for each one — pick which to revert, and the host goes back.

intro.title: Why this exists
intro.body: Every tweak the post-install scripts apply is tracked in a JSON registry at /usr/local/share/proxmenux/installed_tools.json. That registry is what powers the uninstall flow — it shows you the list of optimizations currently applied, and a reversal function that restores the original state for each one (from backup files where possible, or by reinstalling the affected packages).

openMenu.heading: How to open it
openMenu.body: From ProxMenux's main menu, Settings post-install Proxmox → Uninstall optimizations. You will see a checklist of currently applied optimizations — items you have not applied don't show up.
openMenu.imageAlt: Uninstall Optimizations checklist showing items currently applied on the host, with checkboxes to select which to revert

steps.title: Registry and auto-detection
steps.body1: On first run, ProxMenux walks the host looking for fingerprint files (e.g. /etc/sysctl.d/99-memory.conf, /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/99-force-ipv4, haveged package installed, Log2RAM service active…). Anything found is added to the registry as reversible, even if it was applied by an older ProxMenux version that predates the registry.
steps.body2: This migration only runs once. After that, every apply/revert updates the registry directly.

steps.title: Pick what to revert
steps.body1: The checklist shows a human-readable label per item (e.g. Memory Settings Optimization, IOMMU/VFIO PCI Passthrough, Log2RAM (SSD Protection)). Tick the ones you want to reverse. Nothing you don't tick will be touched.

steps.title: Reversal runs
steps.body1: For each selected item, ProxMenux calls its matching uninstall function. Most reversals follow one of three patterns:
items: Backup-based — restore a .bak captured at apply time (bashrc, logrotate.conf, journald.conf, GRUB/kernel cmdline).
Delete-the-config — remove ProxMenux's /etc/sysctl.d/99-*.conf, /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/99-*, or systemd unit, then reload.
Package reinstall — for UI changes like the subscription banner, reinstall the upstream packages with --force-confnew to restore shipped configuration.
steps.body2: Each reversal logs its progress. Items that require a reboot (VFIO, persistent interface names) set a flag that triggers the reboot prompt at the end.

steps.title: Reboot if needed
steps.body1: If any reversed item modified kernel parameters, kernel modules, or network naming, you'll be offered a reboot. Otherwise the changes are live immediately.

reversible.heading: What is reversible
reversible.intro: Every optimization the post-install scripts apply has a matching uninstaller. Grouped here by area:

items.restores: Reinstalls pve-manager, proxmox-widget-toolkit, libjs-extjs and libpve-http-server-perl with force-confnew to restore the original UI files. Also clears cached .js / .gz copies.
Removes /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/99-disable-translations. APT will download language packages again.
Removes /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/99-force-ipv4.
Removes /etc/sysctl.d/99-memory.conf and reloads sysctl.
Removes /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernelpanic.conf.
Removes /etc/sysctl.d/99-maxwatches.conf, 99-maxkeys.conf, 99-swap.conf, 99-fs.conf and /etc/security/limits.d/99-limits.conf. Reverts PAM limits and systemd DefaultLimitNOFILE.
Removes /etc/sysctl.d/99-network.conf and the proxmenux-fwbr-tune.service unit. Reloads sysctl and systemd.
Removes every .link file from /etc/systemd/network/. Interface names return to systemd's default behaviour on next reboot.
Rewrites /etc/systemd/journald.conf with vanilla defaults and restarts systemd-journald.
Restores /etc/logrotate.conf from the .bak file captured before the change.
Stops and disables the service and timer. Purges cron jobs, systemd units, binaries, config files and the /var/log.hdd directory. Also uninstalls the apt package if it was installed that way.

Reads /usr/local/share/proxmenux/zfs_autotrim_pools (the list of pools ProxMenux actually changed) and runs zpool set autotrim=off on each one. Pools you set autotrim on manually before ProxMenux ran are not touched.
Restores /root/.bashrc from the .bak backup. If no backup exists, removes the PMX_CORE_BASHRC block by markers.
Removes the binary, config directory, update-motd hook and the bashrc block. Purges the apt package if installed.
Removes the binary, profile.d entry and the alias block in bashrc/profile.

Removes vfio modules from /etc/modules, the nouveau / radeon / nvidia blacklist entries, and intel_iommu=on / amd_iommu=on / iommu=pt / pcie_acs_override parameters from /etc/kernel/cmdline (ZFS) or GRUB. Rebuilds initramfs.
Removes idle=nomwait from kernel cmdline (ZFS) or GRUB, and the ignore_msrs / report_ignored_msrs options from /etc/modprobe.d/kvm.conf.
Sets timezone back to UTC (safe default) via timedatectl.
Stops, disables and purges the haveged package.
Disables kexec-pve.service, removes the unit file and the reboot-quick alias, purges kexec-tools.

edge.heading: Edge cases and caveats
edge.packageTitle: Package reinstall touches live Proxmox packages
edge.packageBody: Reverting Subscription Banner Removal reinstalls pve-manager, proxmox-widget-toolkit, libjs-extjs and libpve-http-server-perl with --force-confnew. This is generally safe but does touch the running web UI — refresh your browser afterwards, and expect a few seconds of reconnection. Don't run this in the middle of a migration or clone operation.
edge.rebootTitle: Persistent names and VFIO need a reboot
edge.rebootBody: Removing the .link files (Persistent Interface Names) and reverting IOMMU/VFIO do not affect the running system — they only matter after a reboot. ProxMenux sets the reboot flag automatically for these.
edge.perItemTitle: You can revert one thing and keep the rest
edge.perItemBody: The uninstaller operates per-item. If you only want to remove Log2RAM but keep the network tuning and bashrc changes, tick only Log2RAM. Nothing else is touched, and the registry is updated accordingly.

inspect.heading: Inspecting the registry manually
inspect.intro: If you want to see what's tracked without opening the menu:
inspect.outro: Each "tool": true entry corresponds to something ProxMenux applied and can reverse. Removing an entry manually is not recommended — always use the menu, which also runs the reversal function instead of just forgetting the change.
inspect.reinstallBody: Reverting an optimization doesn't prevent you from re-applying it later. Open <link>the Post-Install menu</link> again and run either Automated or Customizable — the registry will track the new state.

items.label: Uninstall ProxMenux
items.tail: — different operation: removes ProxMenux itself, not its applied optimizations.
